1:
When the Lord knew that the Pharisees had heard of his growing baptismal ministry, he withdrew from Judea — and the cloud of witnesses focuses its attention on why. The voices are nearly unanimous that this was not flight from danger but prudential restraint: the Latin fathers, particularly Augustine, stress that the dispute about baptism numbers (John 3:26) had inflamed envious hearts, and Christ did not wish to become the occasion of faction before the proper time. The Antiochene Chrysostom adds a deeper note: had Jesus been seized and escaped, the reality of his flesh might have been doubted; concealing himself served the truth of the incarnation.

The Reformed voices (Calvin, Gill) are firm that Jesus's knowledge here is divine omniscience — he needed no informant. Calvin stresses that the motive was not to avoid his Father's will but to fulfill it at the appointed time. The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes) note the textual variant between Lord and Jesus in the best manuscripts, and observe that this clause is the narrative hinge transitioning from John's ministry to the Galilean journey. All streams read the retreat through the lens of providential timing: departure from Judea is not failure but preparation.

The disciples' having administered baptism while Jesus himself did not (v.2) is treated by the Latin fathers — especially Alcuin — as establishing the principle that baptism's power is independent of the minister's greatness, since it belongs entirely to its Author.
2:
The parenthetical correction — though Jesus himself baptized not, but his disciples — receives careful attention. The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes) treat it as correcting the report that reached the Pharisees: multitudes were thronging, disciples assisted, and the report overstated Jesus's personal role. Robertson alone notices the rare Greek particle kaítoige (and yet indeed), which adds a note of gentle surprise: despite all this baptizing activity, Jesus himself was not the agent.

The Reformed voices (Calvin, Gill) draw the theological conclusion that outweighs the grammatical detail: it matters nothing who administers baptism, since the rite belongs entirely to its Author. Calvin puts it memorably — baptism ought not to be estimated by the person of the minister, but its power depends entirely on its Author — and draws the corollary that those baptized by mere men are no less baptized than if Christ had done it himself. This principle will govern the subsequent teaching on worship in spirit and truth (vv.21–24).
3: The witnesses note the simple geography: Jesus left Judea and went into Galilee by way of Samaria. The grammatical-historical commentators concentrate on timing — he had been in Judea roughly six months (Barnes calculates from the harvest note of v.35) and now returns. The motive is consistent with v.1: the Pharisees' growing anger made it unwise to remain. Ellicott notes the textual word again and links this departure back to the first Galilean journey in John 1:43. Wesley observes simply that Jesus left Judea to shun the effects of their resentment — the witnesses agree that restraint, not cowardice, marks the move.
4:
The phrase he must needs go through Samaria draws the witnesses around the question of necessity. The grammatical-historical voices (Lightfoot, Robertson, Clarke, Wesley) are clear that the necessity is geographical: Samaria lay directly between Judea and Galilee, and Lightfoot cites Josephus to confirm that Galilean pilgrims took this road as a matter of course. Clarke adds cartographic precision — Samaria lay north of Judea, bounded by the Great Sea, Galilee, and the Jordan, so that no going from Galilee to Judea was possible without going through it or a long detour.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry provides background on the Samaritans as descendants of Assyrian colonies who had corrupted the worship of God. Calvin and Ellicott agree in dismissing Augustine's clever explanation (Christ went to Samaria because he had done more good there in a few days than in Judea over months) as oversubtle; the text asserts geographical compulsion. Yet Ellicott captures a subtle harmony: both accounts are true — it was necessary by road, and it was necessary by divine appointment.
5:
The witnesses anchor the scene geographically and historically. The grammatical-historical commentators devote most of their attention to identifying Sychar — almost unanimously with ancient Shechem (Sichem), located between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim. Barnes traces its history through Genesis 33:18 and Joshua 21:21; Lightfoot notes Jacob's gift connects to Genesis 48:22 and Genesis 49:22. Robertson flags an ancient debate over whether the name means a term of reproach (cf. Hab 2:18; Isa 28:1), though the identification with Shechem is nearly settled.

Calvin notes that Jerome preferred Shechem, believing Sychar to be a scribal error, but concludes the form was already in common Johannine-era use. Wesley summarizes: formerly called Sichem or Shechem; Jacob gave it on his death bed (Gen 48:22). All streams agree that the rich patriarchal associations of this location — Jacob, Joseph, the tribal land-grant — form the theological backdrop for the encounter about to unfold at Jacob's well.
6:
The cloud concentrates on two details: the identification of Jacob's well and the humanity of Jesus. Robertson draws a careful linguistic distinction: the well is called pēgē (spring) in v.6 and v.14 but phrear (cistern, pit) in v.11 and v.12 — the well is really a deep cistern dug in a land that otherwise has natural springs, a detail that heightens the woman's question about living water. Ellicott confirms that Jacob's well is one of the few sacred sites about which all travellers ancient and modern agree; it lies where the valley turns westward between Ebal and Gerizim.

On Jesus's weariness — wearied as he was from his journey — the witnesses read a clear testimony to genuine human exhaustion. Robertson notes the perfect active participle of kopiaō — he had grown weary by the journey's accumulated labor. The hour is noon, the hottest part of the day. Ellicott reads in sitting thus on the well the mark of a firsthand witness: someone present noted his posture and remembered the time. The humanity of the incarnate Word, weary at a roadside well in the heat of the day, is the setting into which he will speak of water that springs up to eternal life.
7:
The arrival of the Samaritan woman is the hinge-point of the chapter, and the witnesses are careful about both manner and motive. The Antiochene voices — Chrysostom and Theophylact — emphasise that Jesus did not come with a plan to speak to a Samaritan woman; rather, he would not send her away when she appeared. His thirst was physical, born of the noon heat, and he used the moment. Theophylact draws the pastoral inference: Jesus thirsts after that woman's faith as much as for water. Chrysostom adds a note of condescension in the best theological sense — the Lord of heaven stoops to ask a Samaritan for water.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke) are precise: the woman is from the Samaritan country, not the city of Samaria nine miles away. Clarke notes the parallel to Genesis 24:11 and Exodus 2:16 — drawing water at the well was the prescribed role of women, and Jewish tradition said men who wished wives went to the well. The Reformed Calvin reads the scene as Jesus availing himself of the opportunity of instruction that thirst happened to provide, without pretext or contrivance. Wesley observes that in this single conversation Jesus brought her to knowledge the apostles were so long in attaining.
8: The parenthetical detail that the disciples were away buying food explains how Jesus came to be alone with the woman and underscores the providential privacy. The grammatical-historical witnesses (Robertson, Gill, Ellicott) treat it straightforwardly: they had gone on ahead to Sychar, and Jesus was left without means to draw water — which explains why he asks the woman's help. Robertson notes the past-perfect tense: they had already gone before she arrived. Lightfoot raises a pointed irony: the disciples were gone to buy food from Samaritans, yet Jewish law frowned on even a mouthful of Samaritan food as defiling. Wesley notes simply: for his disciples were gone — else he needed not have asked her. The absence of the disciples is both literary explanation and narrative space in which the deepest evangelistic exchange of the Gospel takes place.
9:
The woman's retort — How is it that thou, being a Jew, askest drink of me, a woman of Samaria? — exposes the full social gulf across which Jesus is speaking. The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Barnes, Clarke) explain the historical enmity: the Samaritans were a mixed people descended from colonists Shalmaneser settled in the land after the ten tribes' captivity, who combined fear of God with worship of their own gods (2 Kgs 17:33). Lightfoot, most exact in rabbinic lore, argues that the clause about no dealings somewhat overstates the Greek syngchraomai: Jews and Samaritans had commercial contact but no social friendship.

The Reformed Calvin reads the exchange as a woman retorting the contempt her nation received, and notes that the Samaritans — as a corrupted people who had corrupted God's worship — were despised in return. Wesley reads her words as open simplicity rather than hostility. Ellicott detects a sardonic edge in the Greek — the emphatic pronoun (thou, a Jew) asking her (a Samaritan woman) — and notes that in this verse it is the religious and national position as Samaritan that stands front and center. The woman's question is the social context into which Jesus will pour the teaching about living water.
10:
Jesus's pivot — If thou knewest the gift of God, and who it is that saith to thee, Give me to drink — is the first announcement of the chapter's central theme. The grammatical-historical commentators debate what the gift of God denotes. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott identify it as Christ himself, the free gift of John 3:16 and 2 Cor 9:15 — the one whose identity the woman does not yet know. Calvin reads the gift as the favorable opportunity being afforded to her by providence at this moment. Both readings converge: whatever the grammar, the verse opens the call to ask from the one who gives.

The Reformed voices (Calvin, Gill) are struck by the condescension: what was there in this wretched creature that should entice Christ to bestow his grace upon her? She is a Samaritan, a woman, morally compromised — and yet he presents himself as her Teacher. Wesley brings the exchange to its Wesleyan resolution: the stress lies on thou wouldst have asked of him. The structure of grace is knowing who gives, and then asking. Robertson identifies living water (hydōr zōn) as naturally ambiguous: in ordinary Greek it means running, spring water as opposed to stagnant well-water — and the woman will take it so (v.11) — but Jesus means the spiritual water that gives life eternal.
11:
The woman's reply — Sir, thou hast nothing to draw with, and the well is deep: whence then hast thou that living water? — is a perfectly natural misunderstanding. The grammatical-historical voices (Robertson, Lightfoot, Clarke) note the practical realities: travellers in the East carried their own leather buckets and cords, since public wells provided no equipment. Robertson tracks the linguistic shift from pēgē (spring) to phrear (cistern, pit) — this well was dug and deep, not a natural spring; the woman's logic is impeccable on her own terms.

Ellicott observes a significant change of tone: the half-sarcastic address has given way to the respectful Sir (Kyrie). Something in Jesus's voice has touched her. Calvin notes that she proceeds to charge him with arrogance — implying he claims to do better than the patriarch — yet does so now with genuine puzzlement rather than contempt. The tension between her literal understanding and the deeper spiritual meaning Jesus intends structures the entire first half of the dialogue, and will not be resolved until v.25.
12:
The woman's implicit challenge — Art thou greater than our father Jacob? — draws the witnesses into the irony at the heart of the encounter. The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke) note that the Greek construction expects the answer No, surely not; yet the entire context will answer Yes. Robertson observes that the Samaritans claimed descent from Jacob through Joseph (the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh), so the appeal to their father Jacob is a claim to patriarchal lineage that is itself historically contested. Barnes is direct: the woman supposes Jesus must be claiming to know a better source than Jacob found — and in fact he is, though not in the literal way she means.

The Reformed Calvin presses the irony: she proceeds to charge him with arrogance in exalting himself above the holy patriarch — yet the comparison she draws will rebound on her. He does not say, I am greater, but Whosoever drinketh of this water shall thirst again (v.13), and the implicit contrast settles the question. The Antiochene tradition (Chrysostom) notes that Jesus answers by showing the inferiority of Jacob's water, not by asserting his own superiority in so many words — teaching by contrast rather than by self-proclamation.
13:
Jesus's counter — Whosoever drinketh of this water shall thirst again — marks the decisive turn of the dialogue. The Antiochene tradition (Chrysostom, Theophylact) reads it as a pedagogy of ascent: the woman is being led by steps from the material to the spiritual. Theophylact notes that the saints receive through grace the seed and principle of good, but they themselves make it grow by cultivation — spiritual water is living and multiplying, unlike well-water that empties and must be refilled.

The grammatical-historical Robertson is at his most precise: he identifies two carefully chosen tenses — pinōn (present participle, the one who keeps on drinking) describes the drinker from this well, who returns again and again, while piē (aorist subjunctive in v.14, whoever shall drink once) describes the one who receives the living water once for all. The physical reality is repetition; the spiritual is finality. Calvin draws the theological conclusion: one water is for the body, and only for a time; while the other feeds the soul and is eternal.
14:
The positive promise — the water that I shall give him shall be in him a well of water springing up into everlasting life — receives the fullest commentary in the chapter. The grammatical-historical voices (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes, Clarke) analyze the language carefully. Robertson notes the double negative ou mē — the strongest possible denial — and the shift from external supply to internal spring: the believer does not merely receive water but has in himself a fountain. Ellicott draws the contrast with v.6: the physical well gives only to those who come and draw; this water is given once and then wells up from within.

The Wesleyan Wesley applies the logic of failure: will never thirst — provided he continue to drink thereof. If ever that thirst returns, it will be the fault of the man, not the water. The promise is unconditional on Christ's side but requires continued appropriation. The Alexandrian Cyril of Jerusalem applies the verse pneumatologically — drink we of living water, springing up into everlasting life — reading the gift as the Holy Spirit bestowed in baptism, connecting the verse to John 7:37–39.
15:
The woman's request — Sir, give me this water, that I thirst not, neither come hither to draw — exposes the depth of misunderstanding still remaining. The grammatical-historical commentators agree that she is still reading literally: she wants freedom from the daily drudgery of water-carrying. Barnes observes that it shows how slow sinners are to understand the doctrines of religion, while Clarke notes that her curiosity has been aroused even if her comprehension has not yet been formed — which was precisely Christ's design, that he might have her mind properly prepared to receive the great truths which he was about to announce.

Robertson detects a half-ironic note in her reference to this water — she is gesturing at the well and the whole peculiar conversation, not yet grasping the offer. The Reformed Calvin reads her persistent literalism as evidence that she despises Christ's promises because she has not yet acknowledged his authority: so long as the authority of him who speaks is not acknowledged by us, his doctrine is not permitted to enter. The remedy will come when Jesus pierces her conscience directly in vv.16–18.
16:
The command Go, call thy husband comes as a sudden pivot, and the witnesses all read it as a deliberate change of method rather than a change of subject. The grammatical-historical commentators (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson) observe two imperatives — go (present, durative) and call (aorist, punctual) — and note that Jesus is not deflecting but advancing: the woman cannot receive the living water while her conscience remains blocked by sin. Clarke is direct: Christ spoke to make the woman consider her own state, and to show her that he knew her heart.

The Reformed Calvin reads the shift as supremely pedagogical: when he perceived that jeers and scoffs were her only reply, he resolved to treat her in a different manner and bring her to seriousness. Ellicott adds the image of a well that needs to be dug before the spring can flow — in the depth of her spirit there is a power of life, but it is hidden. Wesley is the most vivid: he strikes directly at her bosom sin. The moment marks the turn from discourse about water to the surgery of the Word on a particular conscience.
17:
The woman's reply — I have no husband — is technically accurate and spiritually evasive. The grammatical-historical Robertson unpacks the ambiguity: the Greek anēr means either man or husband; she had her man, but he was not a legal husband, and her language veils the deceit. Ellicott observes the tonal shift: she is no longer prefacing her words with the sardonic address or the respectful Sir — the stroke of conscience has silenced the social pleasantries. Barnes notes that she feared that if she came with the man she lived with, the truth might be exposed.

The Reformed Calvin draws the pastoral point: we are so intoxicated by our self-love that we are not at all moved by the first wounds that conscience receives. The woman does not leave — she does not respond in anger — and that itself, for Ellicott, is significant: her admiration for him is raised. Wesley notes with characteristic lightness: thou hast well said — we may observe in all our Lord's discourses the utmost weightiness, and yet the utmost courtesy. The commendation of her truth-telling is itself the instrument of deeper exposure.
18:
Jesus's revelation — thou hast had five husbands; and he whom thou now hast is not thy husband — is the supernatural turning-point of the dialogue. The witnesses focus on two things: what this reveals about the woman's past, and what it proves about Jesus's person. On the first, there is a mild debate. Lightfoot reads serial adultery, describing Christ as stopping her mocking mouth with the evidence of her own unchaste conversation. Clarke resists the harsher reading: she may have been legally married to all five and divorced for reasons not amounting to adultery, since Mosaic law required the adulteress's death; the sixth relationship is the one lacking legal standing.

On the second point, all agree: the knowledge is supernatural. Ellicott paints the moment in Johannine colours — it lays bare to her own consciousness the past and present life, the bright days of joy and dark days of sin. Barnes sees that the woman must now recognize that this is no common and ordinary person. Wesley is restrained but precise: whether they were all dead or not, her own conscience now awakened would tell her. The five husbands are less important than what the knowing of them discloses about the one who knows.
19:
The woman's response — Sir, I perceive that thou art a prophet — marks a watershed in the dialogue. The Antiochene Chrysostom emphasizes that she was not offended at Christ's rebuke; she does not leave him; far from it, her admiration for him is raised. This is read as a signal of genuine receptiveness: the wound of conviction has not driven her away but drawn her deeper. Barnes explains the woman's category: she does not yet think he is the Messiah (v.25) but one who knew her heart and life and must therefore have come from God. Robertson notes the progressive verb theōrō — I am beginning to perceive — caught in the act of perception.

The Reformed Calvin reads the sequence as the proper fruit of the whole exchange: having been informed of her sin, she now desires and requests instruction of her own accord. Repentance opens the door for doctrine. Wesley's four words are the most economical summary of the transition: Sir, I perceive — so soon was her heart touched. The recognition of Jesus as prophet is not the end but the threshold of her journey toward recognizing the Messiah himself (v.26).
20:
The woman's question about where to worship — Our fathers worshipped in this mountain (pointing to Gerizim) — is the defining Samaritan theological question, and the witnesses differ on its motive. The grammatical-historical commentators (Barnes, Lightfoot, Robertson) supply the history: the Samaritan temple on Gerizim was built by Sanballat, and the Samaritans cited the patriarchal altars at Shechem (Gen 12:7; Gen 33:20) to justify their Mount. Lightfoot supplies the bitter inter-communal history behind this irreconcilable rivalry.

The Reformed Calvin resists the reading that she is evading the personal challenge: she passes from what is particular to what is general, and, having been informed of her sin, wishes to be generally instructed about religion. In other words, the conviction of sin has made her ask the deepest religious question she knows how to ask. Ellicott and Wesley support this charitable reading: the question is genuine, prompted by the recognition that she is speaking to someone qualified to resolve it. The woman is moving, however haltingly, toward the most momentous exchange of vv.21–26.
21:
Jesus opens his answer to the worship question with the singular address Believe me — unique in the Gospel, as Wesley notes, used here to a Samaritan where he would normally say I say unto you to Jews. The witnesses read it as marking the solemnity of what follows. Robertson identifies the phrase as substituting for the usual double Amen, amen and notes its unusual force. The Antiochene Chrysostom seizes on the priority of faith: everywhere we have need of faith, the mother of blessings — only by faith can this teaching about spiritual worship be received.

The announcement — the hour cometh, when ye shall neither in this mountain, nor yet at Jerusalem, worship the Father — is read by the Reformed Calvin as setting aside the entire ceremonial provision of the Mosaic law: when he says the hour is at hand when there shall be no peculiar and fixed place for worship, he means that what Moses delivered was only for a time. Robertson and Ellicott note the contrast with v.23: here the coming is future (local worship not yet abolished), there it is both coming and now is (spiritual independence of place is called for immediately).
22:
The frank comparison — Ye worship ye know not what; we know what we worship: for salvation is of the Jews — is handled with care by the witnesses. The grammatical-historical voices (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke, Wesley) are direct: the Samaritans, by rejecting the Prophets and the Psalms, cut themselves off from the fuller knowledge of God and so worshipped in ignorance. Robertson cites Acts 17:23 for the same formula of unknown worship. The clause salvation is of the Jews means, as all agree, that the Messiah comes from and through Israel — Jesus speaks here as a Jew identifying with his own people.

The Reformed Calvin explains what knowing in worship means: the Samaritans worshipped in a corrupted form, mixing elements from foreign religion with Mosaic fragments they accepted. The declaration of Jewish superiority is not racial pride but theological fact: God had deposited the fullness of revelation in Israel. Yet Calvin adds the critical transition: this very superiority is about to be relativized by the announcement of spiritual worship (v.23), for the hour is coming when neither Gerizim nor Jerusalem will matter.
23:
The climax of the worship discourse — the true worshippers shall worship the Father in spirit and in truth — is the verse the whole exchange has been building toward. The grammatical-historical commentators focus on the addition and now is: unlike v.21, where the future hour was purely future, here the spiritual emancipation from place is already present. Robertson contrasts the two verses precisely: local worship was not abolished, but spiritual independence of place was called for at once. Ellicott adds that a band of true worshippers was being gathered, and some were even there — the Samaritan woman herself is the first example.

The Reformed Calvin treats the verse as the abolition of Mosaic externalism: Christ puts an end to the ceremonies and substitutes the worship of the spirit and heart. The Wesleyan Wesley applies it geographically and temporally: at all times and in all places. The Alexandrian Cyril of Jerusalem reads the verse pneumatologically, identifying the Spirit in in spirit and truth with the Holy Spirit given in baptism, drawing the connection to John 3:5. All streams converge on the single point that the new age of worship has arrived in the person of Jesus.
24:
God is Spirit is one of the great ontological declarations of the Gospel, and the witnesses treat it accordingly. The grammatical-historical Robertson is careful: God is Spirit (anarthrous in Greek, like God is Light in 1 John 1:5 and God is Love in 1 John 4:8) is a predicate of nature, not of identity — it cannot be reversed to Spirit is God. The non-corporeality of God is asserted along with his personality; he is not material, not composed of parts, not visible or locally bounded. Ellicott notes that this doctrine had particular prominence in Samaritan tradition, making the argument directly relevant to the woman's own background.

The Reformed Calvin grounds the argument theologically: since men are flesh, we ought not to wonder if they take delight in things corresponding to their own disposition — hence ceremonialism and external worship. But because God is spirit, he requires worship suited to his nature. Clarke calls the verse one of the first, the greatest, the most sublime, and necessary truths in the compass of nature. The Wesleyan Wesley identifies the practical implication: our worship should be suitable to his nature — faith, hope, and love. The Alexandrian Cyril of Jerusalem presses the connection to the Spirit of baptism: true worship is enabled by the indwelling Spirit who corresponds to the Spirit-nature of God himself.
25:
The woman's confession — I know that Messias cometh, which is called Christ: when he is come, he will tell us all things — is the hinge between her awakening and Jesus's self-disclosure. The grammatical-historical witnesses all note the Samaritan Messianic expectation. Barnes explains it from the Pentateuch, which the Samaritans did accept: Deuteronomy 18:15–18 promises a prophet like Moses who would tell us all things. Robertson adds that Simon Magus later exploited this same expectation (Acts 8:9–10). Lightfoot devotes a careful note to the question of how the Samaritans could know the title Messiah without the Prophets, concluding the Pentateuch alone was sufficient.

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the woman's tone as astonishment at the heights to which the teaching has risen: she cannot process it all herself and falls back on the Messiah as the one who will tell us all things — which is precisely what Jesus has been doing. Calvin reads her as genuinely humble: although religion among the Samaritans was corrupted, yet some principles taken from the Law were impressed on their minds. Wesley sees the woman's words as spoken with joy for what she had already learned, and desire of fuller instruction. The Messianic expectation, however imperfectly formed, is what makes the forthcoming self-disclosure (v.26) possible.
26:
I that speak unto thee am he — Jesus's declaration of Messianic identity is the climax of the encounter, and the witnesses mark it as unprecedented. Barnes observes that this was the first time that he openly professed it and that he did not yet do so plainly to the Jews, because it would have excited envy and opposition; but in Samaria nothing was to be feared from it. Clarke notes the directness: to his own countrymen he never spoke in such direct terms concerning himself until near his death.

Robertson identifies the Greek construction — Egō eimi, ho lalōn soi (I am he, the one speaking to thee) — as a full self-identification in plain language. Ellicott notes that the announcement being made had been implicit in everything from the gift of God (v.10) onward: the solution of the problems the woman connects with the Messianic advent is contained in the very words she has heard. Calvin stresses that Jesus presents himself as her Teacher, in compliance with the expectation which she had formed. Wesley observes practical wisdom: the Jews would have used so plain a declaration to ignite armed revolt; the Samaritan woman receives it quietly and turns immediately to witness (vv.28–29).
27:
The return of the disciples is timed perfectly — Chrysostom notes it was opportunely at the moment the teaching was complete. The witnesses focus on the disciples' astonishment and restraint. Chrysostom reads the marvel as directed at the humility and condescension of Christ: stooping to converse with a poor Samaritan woman was the offense, not primarily that she was Samaritan. Lightfoot cites explicit rabbinic materials — the words of the Law were to be burnt rather than taught to a woman — and Ellicott concurs: it was directly contrary to Rabbinic precepts to speak in public with a woman.

The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the imperfect tense ethaumazon: the astonishment of the disciples as they watched Jesus talking with a woman. They stared but said nothing — such respect and reverence had they for him, says Barnes. Origen notices that the woman left her waterpot — she is converted and departs toward a different object. The Antiochene stream takes the waterpot as practical charity (she left it for Jesus and the disciples to drink) and as a symbol of her transformed preoccupation (v.28).
28:
The abandoned waterpot is the detail that catches every witness's attention. The grammatical-historical Robertson reads the ingressive aorist aphēken as the moment of sudden resolution: in her excitement and embarrassment she left it — the waterpot was too large for speed anyway. Barnes frames the action psychologically: her mind was greatly excited; she was disturbed, and hastened to the city in great agitation. Clarke adds the wonder: she was so penetrated with the great truths which Jesus had announced that she forgot her errand to the well, and returned to the city without the water for which she came out. Ellicott notes that the detail of the waterpot left behind is a mark of the presence of him who has related the incidents — the firsthand witness's memory of a very particular thing.

The Reformed Calvin draws the theological principle: this is the nature of faith, that when we have become partakers of eternal life, we wish to bring others to share. The woman has received the gift (v.10) and turns immediately to give it away. Chrysostom marvels at the energy: we require much fervor and uproused zeal, for without these it is impossible to obtain the blessing of the Gospel. The abandoned waterpot is a small concrete image of a soul so struck by the presence of Christ that earthly necessities simply fall from the hand.
29:
The woman's invitation — Come, see a man, which told me all things that ever I did: is not this the Christ? — is a model of apostolic witness, and the witnesses attend to both its form and its content. The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Barnes, Ellicott) note that all things I did slightly overstates the actual exchange — Jesus named five husbands and one present man — but a guilty conscience has filled in the rest. Robertson observes the Greek particle mēti: the question does not assert a firm conclusion but invites the hearers to draw their own. Clarke identifies this as calibrated to the Jewish and Samaritan Messianic criterion that the Messiah would know the secrets of hearts (based on Isa 11:2–3).

The Reformed Calvin reads her modesty as strategic wisdom: she endeavours, according to her feeble capacity, to bring her fellow-citizens to permit themselves to be taught — not claiming authority she does not have, but opening a door. Wesley adds that she does not doubt of it herself, but incites them to make the inquiry. Clarke names her the first apostle of Christ in Samaria — a woman brought the message that male apostles would later confirm.
30: The terse note — then they went out of the city, and came unto him — is handled with characteristic Johannine precision by the grammatical-historical witnesses. Robertson marks the tense contrast: exēlthon is second aorist (effective — they went out at once and in a rush) while ērchonto is imperfect (continuous — they were coming to him, still on the way). This means that the conversation of vv.31–38 with the disciples takes place while the Samaritans are still approaching — giving the harvest metaphor its dramatic point: the fields are whitening as the men walk toward him. Ellicott observes that the woman's alacrity demonstrates the receptive spirit of the Samaritans and the Messianic expectation that made her testimony so effective.
31: The disciples' return with food provides Jesus with another teaching opportunity. The Antiochene Augustine and Theophylact read the disciples' urging as simple care for a weary teacher, not impatience: they all ask him at once, him so fatigued with the journey and heat. Theophylact notes that this shows Jesus was accustomed to receive food from others when offered — not that he who gives food to all flesh needed any assistance, but he received it for the sake of his genuine human nature. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect ērōtōn (kept beseeching him) and flags that Rabbi rather than Master better preserves the Hebrew form the disciples used. Wesley's single note places the scene in its temporal frame: in the meantime — before the people came. The disciples' concern becomes the occasion for the spiritual harvest metaphor that follows.
32:
Jesus's reply — I have meat to eat that ye know not of — reproduces the pattern of the woman's dialogue: a literal misunderstanding by the disciples opens the door for spiritual teaching. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the emphatic pronoun contrast: I have ... ye have not — the spiritual food is uniquely his, for it is the doing of the Father's will. Ellicott notes that the word for meat here (brōsin) is the same as in John 6:27 and John 6:55 — consistently marking spiritual sustenance. Clarke reads the verse as an invitation to apostolic zeal: let the salvation of souls lie nearer your heart than life itself.

The Reformed Calvin refuses to turn the moment into a principle of asceticism or self-mortification: I think it wonderful that, when he is fatigued and hungry, he refuses to eat; for if it be said that he does this for the purpose of instructing us by his example to endure hunger, why then did he not do so always? The refusal to eat is circumstantial, not programmatic — Jesus is seized by the harvest opportunity before him. The food he has is specifically the doing and finishing of the Father's will (v.34).
33: The disciples' puzzlement — Hath any man brought him aught to eat? — mirrors the woman's literal misunderstanding in v.11. The grammatical-historical voices (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke) note the negative particle mē: the question expects no, but the disciples cannot be certain. Clarke finds they recalled precedents of supernatural provision: Elijah fed by ravens at Cherith (1 Kgs 17:4–6) and by the angel (1 Kgs 19:5–6). Barnes notes the only person present was the Samaritan woman — making supernatural provision plausible. The disciples' misunderstanding is theologically charitable — they at least consider miraculous provision — but it misses the point entirely, as the explanation in v.34 shows.
34:
The explanation — My meat is to do the will of him that sent me, and to finish his work — is the Christological spine of the passage. The grammatical-historical Robertson identifies two purpose clauses expressing the Messianic program: that I may do the will and that I may finish the work — not two separate things but the one mission stated from two angles. He notes that Jesus's Messianic consciousness is clear and steady throughout John (5:30; 6:38). Ellicott connects the language to John 17:4 and John 19:30 — I have finished the work / it is finished — so that the word to the disciples in Sychar already points toward Calvary.

The Reformed Calvin refuses to read mere high esteem for God's will into the verse: he means not only that he esteems it very highly, but that there is nothing in which he takes greater delight. He draws the Davidic parallel (Ps 119:97). Clarke draws the ministerial application: let the salvation of souls lie nearer your heart than life itself. Wesley condenses to the essential: my meat — that which satisfies the strongest appetite of my soul.
35:
The harvest metaphor — lift up your eyes, and look on the fields; for they are white already to harvest — is the climax of Jesus's discourse to the disciples. The witnesses divide on whether four months and then cometh harvest is a proverb or a calendar statement. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read it as a proverbial saying about the normal interval between sowing and reaping; Lightfoot treats it as a specific chronological note, calculating that the scene falls in December (four months before the Passover barley-harvest in Nisan). Either way, the application is the same: the physical harvest waits, but the spiritual harvest does not.

Chrysostom and the Antiochene tradition see Jesus reaping as he speaks: the Samaritans streaming toward him across the fields are the whitening harvest. The Samaritans, ripe for the Gospel, covered the ground round about them, Wesley notes. The Reformed Calvin applies the imperative of urgency: when the corn is ripe, the harvest cannot bear delay, for otherwise the grain would fall to the ground. Disciples who see whitening fields and wait four months are failing the harvest. The verse has functioned as a mission-text across every stream of the tradition.
36:
The harvest economics — he that reapeth receiveth wages, and gathereth fruit unto life eternal: that both he that soweth and he that reapeth may rejoice together — are applied by the witnesses as a theology of mission. The grammatical-historical Robertson moves directly to the application: the spiritual harvester can gather his harvest without waiting four months; Jesus is reaping a harvest right now by the conversion of this woman. Ellicott identifies the wages as the joy of gathering others into eternal life — the greatest the heart can know. The joy of sower and reaper together is the distinctive note: unlike earthly farming, where sower and reaper are separated by months, the spiritual harvest brings them together in simultaneous rejoicing.

The Reformed Calvin draws the motivational point: this proves how diligently we ought to devote ourselves to the work of God — the reward is not corruptible or fading. Wesley applies the harvest picture to the Father and Son as joint laborers: as the Father soweth and the Son reapeth, both rejoice together. The verse anticipates the explicit theology of sower and reaper in vv.37–38.
37:
The proverbial saying — one soweth, and another reapeth — is identified by the grammatical-historical commentators (Barnes, Robertson) as a well-known Greek and Hebrew proverb. Its application here is to the relation between the Old Testament prophets (who sowed the seed of Messianic expectation) and the disciples (who will reap the harvest). Wesley characterizes it simply: one is the sower — Christ; another is the reaper — the apostles and succeeding ministers. Robertson emphasizes that in this case the proverb is peculiarly true (alēthinos) in the spiritual realm.

The Reformed Calvin notes the usual pain of the proverb: earthly farmers resent seeing someone else carry off what they labored to produce. But here the apostles do not resent the prophets' priority, because they share the same cause and the same Lord. The proverb is verified in Samaria at this very moment: John the Baptist and the Old Testament prophets sowed; the disciples are about to reap a harvest from a Samaritan city they did nothing to prepare (v.38).
38: The application — I sent you to reap that whereon ye bestowed no labour: other men laboured, and ye are entered into their labours — closes the harvest discourse with an economic theology of mission. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the emphatic egō apestéila (I myself sent) and places the verse in the context of the Baptist's preparatory work (John 3:23–36) and the prophets before him. Barnes identifies other men as the prophets who long laboured to prepare the way for the great harvest which is now to be gathered in. Ellicott reads the verse as having wide meaning — Jesus is ever the Sower, and all others are more or less fully reapers; the more truly anyone reaps, the more they become likened unto him. The principle explains why the disciples have success in Samaria without effort — they are inheriting centuries of prophetic and Baptist preparation.
39:
The result — many of the Samaritans of that city believed on him for the saying of the woman — is one of the most remarkable conversion accounts in the Gospels. Barnes notes the extraordinary character of the success: from a single conversation, in circumstances little likely to be attended with such results, many sinners were converted. Robertson praises the woman's method: she bore her witness clearly and with discretion; she told enough to bring her neighbours to Christ. Clarke names her the first apostle of Christ in Samaria — a woman brought the message that male apostles would later confirm.

The Alexandrian Origen, drawing the line of the harvest image, sees the field covered with sheaves at the moment of the Samaritans' coming. The Reformed Calvin is careful about the nature of this first faith: it is inaccurately called faith in its fullest sense — these were favorably disposed toward Christ on the woman's word rather than having fully trusted him. Their fuller faith will come in vv.41–42 after personal encounter. But even this initial receptiveness is marked as a contrast to Jewish rejection: they refused the witness of a great prophet; these accept the witness of a woman (Ellicott).
40: The Samaritans' invitation for Jesus to remain and his two-day stay draw a telling contrast with Jewish reception. Chrysostom opens his homily with the irony: nothing is worse than envy and malice, and this it was that showed the Jews inferior to the Samaritans despite all their prophetic advantage. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that Jesus accepted the hospitality of Samaria and abode with them for two days — a most unexpected experience given the feeling between Jews and Samaritans. Clarke adds the telling detail: we are not told that he wrought any miracles among them — the Samaritans believed his word alone, without signs. The two-day stay represents, as Ellicott observes, an ongoing revival whose full content the Gospel leaves for the imagination.
41: The growth note — and many more believed because of his own word — is handled economically by the witnesses. Robertson marks the Greek superlative carefully: pollō pleiones, more by much, comparing the many of v.39 (who believed on the woman's word) with the many more who now believe on Jesus's own direct teaching. Ellicott notes the veil drawn over the two days' content: what questions they must have asked; what truths he must have taught — John leaves the richness of that stay to the imagination. Robertson comments that Jesus was reaping more rapidly than the woman did; but all were rejoicing that so many believed. Calvin draws the practical lesson: we see that from his compliance with their wish much fruit was reaped — we ought never to refrain from working when we have an opportunity.
42:
The climax of the Samaritan episode — now we believe, not because of thy saying: for we have heard him ourselves, and know that this is indeed the Christ, the Saviour of the world — is the fullest Christological confession in the chapter. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws the contrast between the woman's laliā (talk, testimony, narrative) and the Samaritans' direct hearing of Jesus himself: no longer because of thy talk — good and effective as that was — but we ourselves have heard. The woman's witness was necessary and irreplaceable as the bridge, but personal encounter with Christ is of a different order.

The title Saviour of the world (sōtēr tou kosmou) is received as the natural and final confession of men who have spent two days in his presence. Ellicott observes the scope: Saviour of the world, not merely of Israel or of Samaria, is the universal title that transcends both Gerizim and Jerusalem. Calvin notes that the progression is exactly what faith always does: the word of man leads us to the word of God; having heard him, we go farther. The woman had been the instrument; Christ himself is the object. The Samaritan episode ends where it was always heading: the confession of the world's Saviour in a place every Jew would have considered unlikely.
43:
The transition — after two days he departed thence, and went into Galilee: for Jesus himself testified, that a prophet hath no honour in his own country — creates an apparent paradox: the next verse (v.45) says the Galileans received him. The witnesses resolve it carefully. The grammatical-historical commentators (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke, Wesley) argue that his own country means specifically Nazareth, which the narrative deliberately avoids: Jesus goes to Cana, not Nazareth. Robertson notes that the proverb is quoted by John as a well-known saying often used by Jesus in various forms (Mk 6:4; Mt 13:57; Lk 4:24).

The Latin fathers, particularly Augustine, note the contrast with Samaria: in Judea and in his home region his reception was most contested; in Samaria strangers believed without miracles. Henry draws the lesson for ministry: even the most fruitful worker will find least honor closest to home. Augustine of Hippo's homily turns the paradox into a meditation on true vs. shallow honor: the Galileans received him based on miracles seen at Jerusalem, not on the living-water teaching of vv.10–14.
44: The verse is the Evangelist's explanatory note, and the witnesses treat it accordingly. Robertson reads it as John's own quotation of a proverbial saying well-known from Jesus's use elsewhere, not necessarily spoken by Jesus on this occasion. Barnes notes that the application is to Nazareth specifically, explaining why Jesus went to Cana rather than home. Ellicott is most explicit about the logic: the common geographical solution, that his own country means Judea, has no authority of the text. The proverb does not explain why Jesus went to Galilee but why he did not go to Nazareth in Galilee. Wesley simply cross-references Matthew 13:57. The verse reinforces the pattern established by the Samaritan episode: those most distant from religious privilege often receive most readily.
45: The Galilean reception — the Galileans received him, having seen all the things he did at Jerusalem at the feast: for they also went unto the feast — is treated by the witnesses as a sign-based faith, thinner than the Samaritan word-based faith. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the participle heōrakotes (perfect active, having seen), pointing back to the Jerusalem miracles of John 2:23 which form the backdrop of their welcome. Barnes observes that this welcome is faith based on miracles — not bad, but comparatively shallow when set beside the Samaritan response. Calvin adds the pastoral caution: whether or not this honor was of long duration we have not the means of determining; for there is nothing to which men are more prone than forgetfulness of the gifts of God. Ellicott notes that Jesus had evidently anticipated a quiet arrival but found the Galileans waiting for him.
46:
The scene returns to Cana — where he made the water wine — and a new suppliant arrives. The grammatical-historical commentators (Barnes, Lightfoot, Clarke) identify the basilikos as an officer of Herod Antipas's court, likely resident in Capernaum about twenty to twenty-five miles away; Robertson notes the word most naturally means a courtier or royal official. The Antiochene Chrysostom notes that Jesus returns to Cana specifically to build on the faith his first miracle had produced there — where he made the water wine recalls that outstanding first miracle.

The Latin father Gregory (in the catena tradition) is the sharpest critic of the nobleman's faith: he doubted, because he asked Jesus to come down — he believed Jesus could heal only by physical presence. The miracle will demonstrate that faith must transcend this spatial limitation. Origen adds a note about the servants who met the nobleman, indicating that the healing was confirmed before his arrival home (v.51).
47:
The nobleman's journey from Capernaum to Cana — twenty miles — demonstrates the depth of a father's anxiety. The grammatical-historical Barnes reads it positively: though high in office, yet he did not refuse to go personally to Jesus to ask his aid; he felt as a father. Robertson notes that the report of Jesus's return to Galilee spread rapidly across the twenty-mile distance. Adam Clarke sees practical wisdom in Jesus's decision not to accompany him: had Jesus gone to Capernaum with the nobleman, the nobleman's faith would have remained dependent on physical proximity; refusing to go was the surgical correction of a deeper defect.

The Reformed Calvin identifies the structural problem with this faith: he views the power of Christ as inseparably connected with his bodily presence, from which it is evident how ignorant he was. The request — come down ere my child die — is a genuine and urgent prayer, not a criticism; but it reveals the limitation that the miracle will correct. Wesley notes simply: come down — for Cana stood much higher than Capernaum. The geographical detail anchors the narrative; the theological detail is the father's assumption that healing requires presence.
48:
The rebuke — Except ye see signs and wonders, ye will not believe — is directed to the Galilean crowd, not only to the nobleman. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that the plural hymeis extends the charge beyond this one man to the class he represents. Barnes makes the comparison explicit: the Samaritans had believed without any miracle; the Galileans were less disposed to believe than even they were. Ellicott identifies the audience as Jews, since there is no indication the nobleman was otherwise. The rebuke contrasts sharply with the Samaritan receptiveness that preceded it — there Jesus gave no miracles; here miracles are demanded.

The Wesleyan Clarke offers a charitable reading: our Lord does not tell this man that he has no faith, but that he has not enough. If he had had none, he would not have come from Capernaum to Cana; if he had had enough, he would have been content with a word. The rebuke is surgical — aimed at the weakness, not at destroying what is genuine. Wesley's summary: unless ye see signs and wonders — although the Samaritans believed without them. The contrast of Samaritan word-faith and Galilean sign-dependent faith reaches its sharpest point here.
49:
The nobleman's response to the rebuke is not argument but persevering urgency: Sir, come down ere my child die. Barnes observes that the earnestness of the nobleman evinces the deep and tender anxiety of a father — so anxious was he for his son that he was not willing that Jesus should delay a moment. Clarke detects a note of hurt: it is difficult for a proud man or a man in office to humble himself — but this father does it. Ellicott reads the moment as the birth-pang of deeper faith: human sorrow is the birth-pang of faith; the sense of utter powerlessness leads the soul to cast itself on the Strong One.

The Reformed Calvin is careful: Christ did not intend his rebuke as a final rejection but only to correct his error — the perseverance of the prayer demonstrates that the rebuke has not destroyed what was there but has left room for growth. Robertson's tenses mark the urgency: katábēthi (second aorist imperative) — come down at once; prin apothanein to paidion mou — before my child dies. The vocabulary is that of a man racing against time.
50:
The healing word — Go thy way; thy son liveth — is the decisive action of the narrative. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the Johannine formula zē (is living, and will not die as feared) and reads it as a word too good and gracious to be true — the absent treatment, without the cure being absent. Barnes frames the moment theologically: Jesus not only restores health but gives evidence that his power is not bound by physical proximity. Adam Clarke reads Christ's refusal to go as pedagogically necessary: in order to destroy his unbelief at once, he heals him at a distance.

The Reformed Calvin is moved by the combination of condescension and power: the astonishing kindness of Christ, that he bears with the man's ignorance, and stretches his power beyond what had been expected. He requested that Christ would come to the place; he receives more than he sought — not by going to the place, but by a simple command, Christ gave life to the son when he was on the point of death. The man believed the word that Jesus had spoken before he had any confirmation from his servants.
51: The servants' meeting with the nobleman on the road — thy son liveth — confirms the miracle before he arrives home. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes a grammatical curiosity: the genitive absolute (as he was now going down) is used despite the pronoun being near. He also tracks the tense: the servants report zē (present, is living), the same form Jesus used, implying the healing has already taken effect. Calvin reads the sequence as a demonstration of two correlatives: the effect of faith together with the efficacy of the word — for as Christ, by a word, restores to life this child who was just dying, so in one moment the father, by his faith, regains his son safe and sound. Ellicott paints the psychological journey of the nobleman on that long road: now faith would be strong, and now almost give way; but he travels on with the words Thy son liveth ringing in his heart.
52:
The verification scene — the nobleman inquiring at what hour his son improved — is handled with precision. Robertson notes the unusual Greek phrase kompsoteron eschen (took a turn, got better — a phrase from Arrian used in a medical context) and translates it as he began to mend. The seventh hour is identified by Barnes and others as one o'clock in the afternoon, consistent with the noon heat of v.6 when Jesus sat at the well. Clarke observes the father's psychological state: he began to feel his faith settled and to have all anxiety removed as he methodically compared times.

The Reformed Calvin interprets the inquiry not as natural curiosity but as divine prompting: this was done by a secret impulse from God, that the truth of the miracle might be rendered more conspicuous; for by nature we have an exceedingly wicked disposition to extinguish the light of God's glory. The inquiry is providentially arranged so that the proof will be complete and undeniable. Wesley draws the application: he asked the hour when he amended — the more exactly the works of God are considered, the more faith is increased.
53:
The confirmation — the hour of healing exactly matched the hour of Jesus's word — produces what the witnesses call a deeper or fuller faith. Robertson marks the aorist episteusen autos (himself believed) and distinguishes it from the trust of v.50: in v.50 the man believed Jesus's word; here he believes in Jesus himself, the absolute use of pisteuō that John consistently uses for complete commitment. The Reformed Calvin addresses the apparent contradiction directly: when the Evangelist says before that the father believed (v.50), he means he gave credit to the saying of Christ; but now he is speaking of a completely different class of faith — such as is the true conversion of a man to God.

The extension to the whole house is treated by Clarke as a full family conversion to the doctrine of the manifested Messiah. Ellicott paints the psychological movement: the nobleman was not mistaken, then — the hours matched, the power was real, the words were true — and from this personal verification emerges whole-life faith. The nobleman's story is a compressed picture of the journey from sign-faith to word-faith to full Christological trust.
54:
The Evangelist's note — this is again the second miracle that Jesus did, when he was come out of Judea into Galilee — closes the chapter with a numerological marker. The grammatical-historical Robertson is precise: this is not the second miracle of Jesus's entire ministry (many were worked at Jerusalem) but the second he performed at Cana after returning to Galilee from Judea. Clarke confirms: the first miracle in Cana was performed just after his baptism; this second took place after his arrival from Jerusalem. Gill agrees: the qualifier when he was come out of Judea into Galilee restricts the count to the Galilean series. Ellicott adds that in John's language every miracle has its deeper teaching — the numbering signals that signs are not random displays of power but purposeful disclosures of glory.

The Antiochene Chrysostom opens his homily on this verse with a reflection on the preciousness of every detail of Scripture: as a skilled miner would not overlook the smallest gold-bearing seam, so we ought not to pass over one jot or tittle. The second Cana miracle closes a narrative arc that began with water into wine (John 2:1–11) and now ends with a word that heals at a distance — each miracle expanding the reader's sense of who this one is, and what he can do.
